Purpose and application of the Rules.subtopic: Purpose and application of the Rules
Purpose and application of the Rules.front: What is the primary purpose of the SRA Accounts Rules 2019?
Purpose and application of the Rules.answer:
The SRA Accounts Rules 2019 exist to protect client money and assets. They require firms to keep client money separate from business money, maintain accurate records, and safeguard clients against loss through theft, negligence, or insolvency. The overarching duty is to ensure that client money is not used to benefit the firm and is held safely until required.

⚠️ The Rules apply to all regulated firms and individuals handling client money in connection with legal services — breach constitutes professional misconduct.
Purpose and application of the Rules.summary_line: SRA ACCOUNTS RULES 2019 · CLIENT PROTECTION · SEPARATION · PROFESSIONAL MISCONDUCT

Purpose and application of the Rules.subtopic: Purpose and application of the Rules
Purpose and application of the Rules.front: What is the framework of core duties imposed by the SRA Accounts Rules 2019?
Purpose and application of the Rules.answer:
The Rules impose four core duties:

1. keep client money separate from office money in a designated client account
2. pay client money into the client account promptly
3. only withdraw client money for permitted purposes
4. maintain proper accounting records.

The Rules are binding; supplementary SRA guidance provides practical detail but does not override the Rules. Breach can constitute professional misconduct regardless of intention.
Purpose and application of the Rules.summary_line: FOUR CORE DUTIES · SEPARATION · PROMPT PAYMENT · PERMITTED WITHDRAWAL · RECORDS

Purpose and application of the Rules.subtopic: Purpose and application of the Rules
Purpose and application of the Rules.front: A solicitor holds £5,000 from a client to fund anticipated conveyancing disbursements not yet incurred. Which rules apply, and what must the solicitor do?
Purpose and application of the Rules.answer:
This is client money under the SRA Accounts Rules 2019, received in connection with regulated legal services for anticipated disbursements. The solicitor must promptly pay the £5,000 into a designated client account. Money may only be withdrawn when disbursements are actually paid.

⚠️ Do not leave client money sitting in client account beyond the point of need — once a disbursement is not going to be incurred, unused money must be returned to the client promptly.
Purpose and application of the Rules.summary_line: CLIENT MONEY · PROMPT PAYMENT · DISBURSEMENTS · WITHDRAWAL ON PAYMENT · RETURN UNUSED

Purpose and application of the Rules.subtopic: Purpose and application of the Rules
Purpose and application of the Rules.front: What powers does the SRA have to enforce the Accounts Rules, and what sanctions are available for breach?
Purpose and application of the Rules.answer:
The SRA may take regulatory action against any authorised firm or individual who breaches the Accounts Rules. Sanctions range from a written warning and financial penalty to suspension and referral to the Solicitors Disciplinary Tribunal (SDT). The SDT may strike a solicitor off the roll. Where client money is at risk, the SRA has power to intervene — freezing accounts and taking custody of client files.

⚠️ Misuse of client money (even without dishonest intent) is treated as a serious breach and frequently results in SDT proceedings.
Purpose and application of the Rules.summary_line: SRA ENFORCEMENT · SDT · INTERVENTION · FINANCIAL PENALTY · STRIKE OFF · SERIOUS BREACH

Purpose and application of the Rules.subtopic: Purpose and application of the Rules
Purpose and application of the Rules.front: Going deeper: Academic critique of the client/office money distinction
Purpose and application of the Rules.answer: The binary client/office money distinction underpinning the SRA Accounts Rules has attracted sustained academic criticism for its rigidity. Professor Richard Moorhead (UCL) has argued that the bright-line categorisation, while administratively convenient, fails to reflect the complexity of modern legal practice — particularly in multi-party transactions, conditional fee arrangements, and hybrid retainers where the beneficial ownership of funds shifts mid-matter. The Law Society's 2017 Practice Finance Committee report acknowledged that practitioners frequently struggle to classify mixed receipts accurately in real time, and that the 14-day transfer window (rule 4.2) is a crude proxy for a more nuanced temporal analysis. An unresolved tension remains: the Rules prioritise systemic protection of the client fund as a whole over granular accuracy at the individual ledger level. This produces anomalies — a firm that routinely holds trivial office money in the client account for days may face the same regulatory sanction as one that deliberately misappropriates funds.

Purpose and application of the Rules.subtopic: Purpose and application of the Rules
Purpose and application of the Rules.front: Going deeper: History of the Rules — 2011 to 2019 reform
Purpose and application of the Rules.answer: The 2011 Accounts Rules were a codification of longstanding practice under successive iterations of the Solicitors Accounts Rules dating from 1945. They were notable for their prescriptive detail — over 50 rules specifying exact procedures for ledger maintenance, interest calculation, and reporting. The Law Society's 2013 review, followed by the SRA's own Regulatory Reform Programme, identified three systemic criticisms: (i) the rules were too granular to allow firms to adapt controls to their own risk profile; (ii) compliance cost was disproportionately high for smaller firms; and (iii) the prescriptive format did not accommodate emerging fintech models (including early TPMA concepts). The 2019 Rules, which came into force on 25 November 2019, represented a deliberate shift to outcomes-focused regulation — reducing the rule count significantly and replacing many procedural requirements with principles. Critics including the Law Society's Small Firms Division argued the reform transferred regulatory burden to firms by requiring them to develop bespoke compliance systems, and that outcomes-focused rules are harder for junior staff to follow than bright-line prescriptions. The tension between prescriptive certainty and outcomes-based flexibility remains unresolved and is likely to inform any future reform cycle.

Purpose and application of the Rules.subtopic: Purpose and application of the Rules
Purpose and application of the Rules.front: Going deeper: Comparative — English vs Scottish Law Society client account model
Purpose and application of the Rules.answer: England and Wales and Scotland operate distinct client money regimes reflecting their different professional regulatory structures. The Law Society of Scotland's 2018 Practice Rules require Scottish solicitors to hold client money in a pooled client account held at a bank or building society on a designated basis — broadly similar to the English position under the SRA Accounts Rules 2019. However, key differences exist. First, Scottish rules historically required a fixed minimum interest rate to be paid on client balances exceeding a specified threshold, rather than the English 'fair sum' standard — providing clients with greater certainty but less flexibility for firms. Second, the Scottish Master Policy for professional indemnity insurance incorporates client account protections more tightly than the English minimum terms. Third, the Scottish rules impose an obligation to report to the Law Society of Scotland where a practitioner suspects a colleague of client account irregularity — a mandatory peer-reporting obligation that has no direct equivalent in English practice. Professor Alan Paterson (Strathclyde) has argued that the Scottish model, by mandating clearer interest obligations and peer-reporting duties, better reflects the fiduciary character of the solicitor-client relationship, and that the English regime's move toward outcomes-focused flexibility has weakened those fiduciary signals.

Who the Rules apply to.front: To whom do the SRA Accounts Rules 2019 apply?
Who the Rules apply to.answer:
The Rules apply to:

1. all SRA-authorised bodies (solicitors' firms, LLPs, companies authorised by the SRA)
2. all individuals authorised by the SRA (solicitors, registered European lawyers, registered foreign lawyers) when acting as managers or employees of an authorised body; and
3. any person who holds or receives client money in connection with regulated legal services provided by an SRA-authorised firm.

⚠️ The Rules do not apply to entities regulated solely by other approved regulators (e.g., BSB-regulated barristers, CLC-regulated conveyancers).
Who the Rules apply to.summary_line: SRA-AUTHORISED BODIES · SOLICITORS · RELs · RFLs · NOT BSB/CLC · REGULATED LEGAL SERVICES

Who the Rules apply to.front: A paralegal employed by an SRA-authorised firm handles client money in a conveyancing transaction. Do the SRA Accounts Rules apply to the paralegal?
Who the Rules apply to.answer:
Yes. The Rules apply to any person who holds or receives client money in connection with regulated legal services provided by an SRA-authorised body — including non-solicitor employees such as paralegals, cashiers, and legal assistants. The firm bears ultimate regulatory responsibility and cannot delegate compliance to unqualified staff.

⚠️ The firm's managers and principals are accountable for ensuring all staff handling client money do so in accordance with the Rules.
Who the Rules apply to.summary_line: PARALEGAL COVERED · EMPLOYEE · FIRM BEARS RESPONSIBILITY · MANAGERS ACCOUNTABLE · ALL STAFF

Who the Rules apply to.front: A barrister receives money from a client for ongoing advice work. Are the SRA Accounts Rules relevant?
Who the Rules apply to.answer:
No. The SRA Accounts Rules apply only to SRA-authorised bodies and individuals. Barristers are regulated by the Bar Standards Board, which has its own separate money-handling rules. Similarly, licensed conveyancers fall under the CLC.

⚠️ A solicitors' firm that instructs a barrister does not pass its Accounts Rules obligations to the barrister — the firm must continue to comply with its own obligations in respect of any client money it holds.
Who the Rules apply to.summary_line: BARRISTERS = BSB · CLC NOT SRA · SRA-ONLY · OTHER APPROVED REGULATORS · FIRM RETAINS OBLIGATION

Who the Rules apply to.front: Gap-fill: Under the SRA Accounts Rules 2019, the Rules apply to SRA-authorised _______ and to those who hold or receive _______ money in connection with _______ legal services.
Who the Rules apply to.answer:
The Rules apply to SRA-authorised bodies (and authorised individuals as managers/employees), and to those who hold or receive client money in connection with regulated legal services.

⚠️ The trigger is receipt or holding of client money — the Rules bite the moment client money is received, regardless of whether formal instructions have been confirmed.
Who the Rules apply to.summary_line: AUTHORISED BODIES · CLIENT MONEY · REGULATED LEGAL SERVICES · TRIGGER ON RECEIPT

Definition of client money.front: What is the definition of 'client money' under the SRA Accounts Rules 2019?
Definition of client money.answer:
Client money is money held or received by a firm from or on behalf of a client in connection with regulated legal services provided by the firm. It includes: money received as a payment on account of costs or disbursements not yet incurred; money received from a third party on behalf of a client (e.g., damages, insurance proceeds); and money received pending completion of a transaction.

⚠️ The word 'client' does not limit client money to money paid by the client — money paid by a third party on behalf of a client is still client money.
Definition of client money.summary_line: HELD OR RECEIVED · ON BEHALF OF CLIENT · REGULATED LEGAL SERVICES · INCLUDES THIRD PARTY RECEIPTS

Definition of client money.front: A client sends the firm £2,000 'to cover fees.' The firm has not yet delivered a bill. Is this client money?
Definition of client money.answer:
Yes. Money received as a payment on account of costs is client money unless and until a bill has been delivered and the money applied to it. The firm must pay the £2,000 into the client account promptly. It cannot be retained in the office account simply because it relates to anticipated fees.

⚠️ Once a bill is delivered, the firm may transfer the relevant sum from client account to office account — but only after the bill is delivered, not before.
Definition of client money.summary_line: PAYMENT ON ACCOUNT = CLIENT MONEY · CLIENT ACCOUNT · BILL REQUIRED BEFORE TRANSFER · NOT OFFICE MONEY YET

Definition of client money.front: Set out the framework for determining whether money received by a firm is 'client money.'
Definition of client money.answer:
Apply three questions:

1. Is the money held or received by the firm? If it goes directly to a third party and never passes through the firm, it may not be 'held.'
2. Is it from or on behalf of a client in connection with regulated legal services? Money received for non-legal services falls outside scope.
3. Does an exclusion apply (e.g., payment against a delivered bill = office money)? If
1. and
2. are yes and no exclusion applies, it is client money.

⚠️ Where in doubt, treat as client money — the protective default safeguards the client.
Definition of client money.summary_line: FRAMEWORK · HELD OR RECEIVED · ON BEHALF · REGULATED SERVICES · EXCLUSIONS · DEFAULT TO CLIENT MONEY

Definition of client money.front: A defendant's insurer pays £50,000 damages directly to the firm's client account on behalf of the firm's client. Is this client money?
Definition of client money.answer:
Yes. Money received from a third party on behalf of a client is client money under the SRA Accounts Rules 2019, even though the payer (the insurer) is not the firm's client. The money must be held in the client account and may only be withdrawn for purposes authorised by the client — typically, to deduct the firm's costs (after a bill is delivered) and pay the balance to the client.

⚠️ The money belongs beneficially to the client from receipt — the firm holds it on trust and cannot use it for its own purposes.
Definition of client money.summary_line: THIRD PARTY RECEIPT · CLIENT MONEY · HELD ON TRUST · AUTHORISED WITHDRAWAL · BELONGS TO CLIENT

Definition of client money.front: A solicitor acting under a lasting power of attorney manages a client's bank accounts. Is money in those accounts 'client money' under the SRA Accounts Rules?
Definition of client money.answer:
Only if the money relates to regulated legal services the firm provides to the client. Money managed purely in an administrative or personal capacity with no regulated legal service being delivered may fall outside scope. However, where the firm receives or holds such money in the course of providing regulated legal services (e.g., giving legal advice in connection with the LPA), it is client money and must be handled accordingly.

⚠️ Exam trap: 'acting as attorney' does not automatically remove money from scope if legal services are also being provided.
Definition of client money.summary_line: LPA · ATTORNEY CAPACITY · REGULATED LEGAL SERVICES · BORDERLINE · DEFAULT TO CLIENT MONEY

What is not client money (office money,.subtopic: What is not client money (office money, out-of-scope)
What is not client money (office money,.front: What categories of money are expressly not 'client money' under the SRA Accounts Rules 2019?
What is not client money (office money,.answer:
The following are not client money:

1. money paid in response to a properly delivered bill — this belongs to the firm on receipt
2. money received as profit costs that the firm is immediately entitled to (e.g., a fixed fee earned in full on receipt where genuinely agreed)
3. the firm's own business money received for non-client purposes.

These must be held in the office account.

⚠️ Do not confuse a payment on account (client money until a bill is delivered) with a payment against a delivered bill (office money from the moment of receipt).
What is not client money (office money,.summary_line: OFFICE MONEY · DELIVERED BILL · PROFIT COSTS · FIXED FEE EARNED ON RECEIPT · NOT CLIENT MONEY

What is not client money (office money,.subtopic: What is not client money (office money, out-of-scope)
What is not client money (office money,.front: The firm delivers a final bill for £3,000. Two days later, the client pays £3,000. Is this client money?
What is not client money (office money,.answer:
No. Money paid in response to a delivered bill is office money — it is received in payment of a debt owed to the firm and belongs to the firm from the moment of receipt. It must be paid into the office account.

⚠️ If it is mistakenly paid into the client account, it must be transferred to the office account promptly. Contrast with a payment on account received before a bill is delivered — that remains client money until the bill is raised and the appropriate transfer is made.
What is not client money (office money,.summary_line: DELIVERED BILL · OFFICE MONEY · OFFICE ACCOUNT · CONTRAST WITH PAYMENT ON ACCOUNT

What is not client money (office money,.subtopic: What is not client money (office money, out-of-scope)
What is not client money (office money,.front: A tribunal orders a respondent to pay £10,000 compensation directly to the claimant. The money is paid directly to the claimant by the respondent. Must the firm account for it under the SRA Accounts Rules?
What is not client money (office money,.answer:
No. The Rules apply only to money held or received by the firm. If the money is paid directly to the client and never passes through the firm's hands, it is not client money and the Accounts Rules are not engaged.

⚠️ If the money had been paid to the firm on behalf of the client — even briefly — it would be client money and would need to be paid into the client account before onward transmission.
What is not client money (office money,.summary_line: NOT HELD OR RECEIVED · NOT CLIENT MONEY · DIRECT PAYMENT · CONTRAST WITH RECEIPT BY FIRM

What is not client money (office money,.subtopic: What is not client money (office money, out-of-scope)
What is not client money (office money,.front: A solicitor acting as executor of an estate receives estate assets. Is this money subject to the SRA Accounts Rules?
What is not client money (office money,.answer:
This is a grey area. Money received purely in a personal capacity as executor, with no connection to regulated legal services, may fall outside scope. However, where the firm is simultaneously acting as solicitor to the estate and as executor, money received in connection with those legal services is likely client money and subject to the Rules.

⚠️ Exam trap: 'acting as executor' does not automatically take money outside scope if the firm is also providing regulated legal services to the estate — the connection to legal services is the key test.
What is not client money (office money,.summary_line: EXECUTOR · PROBATE · OUT OF SCOPE IF PERSONAL ONLY · REGULATED LEGAL SERVICES = IN SCOPE · GREY AREA

What is not client money (office money,.subtopic: What is not client money (office money, out-of-scope)
What is not client money (office money,.front: A firm and client agree in writing at the outset that any money paid by the client will immediately vest as office money. Is this effective?
What is not client money (office money,.answer:
Generally no. A simple contractual agreement cannot convert a payment on account into office money where the money is not genuinely earned and immediately due. The Accounts Rules cannot be contracted out of unilaterally. A narrow exception exists where money is genuinely received as a fixed fee earned in full on receipt — for example, a flat-fee arrangement where the work is completed at the moment of instruction.

⚠️ A purported agreement to treat advance payments as office money where work is ongoing is ineffective and would leave the firm in breach.
What is not client money (office money,.summary_line: CANNOT CONTRACT OUT · PAYMENT ON ACCOUNT · FIXED FEE EXCEPTION · GENUINELY EARNED · ADVANCE ≠ OFFICE MONEY

Mixed receipts.front: What is a 'mixed receipt' under the SRA Accounts Rules and how must it be handled?
Mixed receipts.answer:
A mixed receipt is a single payment that contains both client money and office money (e.g., one payment covering both disbursements held on behalf of a client and the firm's own fees). The firm must split the payment: pay the client money element into the client account and the office money element into the office account. Where the split cannot be immediately identified, the entire amount should be paid into the client account and the office element transferred out once identified.

⚠️ Do not leave office money sitting in the client account — this breaches the Rules.
Mixed receipts.summary_line: MIXED RECEIPT · SPLIT PROMPTLY · DEFAULT TO CLIENT ACCOUNT · TRANSFER OFFICE ELEMENT OUT · BREACH IF MIXED

Mixed receipts.front: A firm receives £5,000 that is part client money (£3,000 disbursements float) and part fees (£2,000 against a delivered bill). How should it handle this, and within what timeframe?
Mixed receipts.answer:
The £3,000 is client money; the £2,000 is office money (fees against a delivered bill). The firm should pay the full £5,000 into the client account if it cannot split the receipt immediately, then transfer the £2,000 to the office account as soon as the split is confirmed — the SRA expects this within 2 working days of identifying the split. If the split is clear from the outset, the firm may split the receipt directly.

⚠️ Do not delay transferring the office element — leaving it in client account is a breach.
Mixed receipts.summary_line: SPLIT WITHIN 2 WORKING DAYS · DEFAULT TO CLIENT ACCOUNT · TRANSFER £2,000 TO OFFICE · PROMPT

Mixed receipts.front: In a conveyancing transaction, the firm receives a cheque covering the firm's fees (£800 inc. VAT, no bill yet delivered) and anticipated SDLT (£2,500). How should this be handled?
Mixed receipts.answer:
Both elements are client money at this stage — the fees have not been billed, so the £800 is client money (a payment on account), not office money. The £2,500 SDLT is client money held for an anticipated disbursement. The firm should pay the full £3,300 into the client account. Once a bill is delivered for fees, transfer £800 to the office account. Pay SDLT from the client account only when payment to HMRC is due.

⚠️ Do not treat the fee element as office money simply because it is anticipated — no bill, no transfer.
Mixed receipts.summary_line: SDLT = CLIENT MONEY · FEES WITHOUT BILL = CLIENT MONEY · FULL AMOUNT TO CLIENT ACCOUNT · BILL BEFORE TRANSFER

Mixed receipts.front: Why is 'pay the entire mixed receipt into client account' the correct default where the split is unclear?
Mixed receipts.answer:
The default reflects the protective purpose of the Rules: client money held in an office account is at risk if the firm becomes insolvent (it becomes available to creditors). Keeping everything in the client account protects the client while the split is determined. The firm can always transfer the office element out once identified; the reverse — client money in the office account — exposes the client to loss.

⚠️ The firm must not leave the process of identifying and transferring the office element indefinitely — prompt action is still required.
Mixed receipts.summary_line: PROTECTIVE PURPOSE · INSOLVENCY RISK · DEFAULT TO CLIENT ACCOUNT · TRANSFER OFFICE ELEMENT · PROMPT

Residual client balances.front: What obligation arises when a matter concludes and client money remains in the client account?
Residual client balances.answer:
Under the SRA Accounts Rules 2019, a firm must return client money to the client promptly once it is no longer needed for the purpose for which it was held. If the matter has concluded and a balance remains, the firm must take active steps to return it without delay.

⚠️ The obligation is active, not passive — the firm cannot simply leave residual balances accumulating in client account indefinitely. Failure to return promptly is a breach even if no dishonesty is involved.
Residual client balances.summary_line: PROMPT RETURN · MATTER CONCLUDED · ACTIVE OBLIGATION · CANNOT RETAIN · BREACH EVEN WITHOUT DISHONESTY

Residual client balances.front: A firm holds £250 as a residual balance for a former client it cannot locate 18 months after the matter concluded. What steps should it take?
Residual client balances.answer:
The firm must take reasonable steps to locate the client: writing to the last known address, checking public records, contacting any known representatives. If the client cannot be traced, the firm may apply to the SRA for permission to pay the balance to an SRA-nominated charity. Full records of the amount, the client, and every step taken must be kept.

⚠️ The money cannot be absorbed into the office account — that would be misappropriation of client money, which is a serious disciplinary matter.
Residual client balances.summary_line: REASONABLE STEPS TO TRACE · LAST KNOWN ADDRESS · SRA PERMISSION · CHARITY PAYMENT · RECORDS · NOT OFFICE MONEY

Residual client balances.front: Under what conditions may a firm pay a residual client balance to charity under the SRA Accounts Rules?
Residual client balances.answer:
A firm may pay a residual balance to an SRA-nominated charity where:

1. all reasonable steps to return the money to the client have been taken and have failed
2. a reasonable period has elapsed; and
3. the SRA has approved the payment, or the balance falls within de minimis SRA guidance allowing self-certification.

The firm must retain detailed records of the amount, client identity, and all steps taken.

⚠️ Paying to charity without exhausting tracing steps or without SRA approval (where required) is a breach and may amount to misappropriation.
Residual client balances.summary_line: CHARITY PAYMENT · REASONABLE STEPS · SRA APPROVAL · DE MINIMIS SELF-CERTIFICATION · RECORDS REQUIRED

Residual client balances.front: A firm concludes a litigation matter and holds £15 in client account that cannot be returned (former client untraceable). What should it do?
Residual client balances.answer:
The firm should take reasonable steps to trace the client. If unsuccessful, SRA guidance permits payment of small de minimis balances to a charity without specific SRA approval. The firm must still document the steps taken and the amount paid.

⚠️ De minimis does not mean the firm may keep the money — it must still go to charity (not to the firm's profit costs), and proper records must be maintained.
Residual client balances.summary_line: DE MINIMIS · £15 · CHARITY WITHOUT SPECIFIC SRA APPROVAL · DOCUMENT STEPS · NOT TO FIRM

Quistclose Trusts.front: Barclays Bank Ltd v Quistclose Investments Ltd [1970] AC 567
Quistclose Trusts.issue: Whether money lent for a specific purpose and paid into a separate account was held on trust, giving the lender priority over the company's general creditors on insolvency.
Quistclose Trusts.rule: Where money is lent for a specific purpose and paid into a separate account, a primary trust arises in favour of that purpose. If the purpose fails, a secondary resulting trust arises in favour of the lender. The money never becomes part of the borrower's general assets.
Quistclose Trusts.application: Rolls Razor Ltd was insolvent but wished to pay a declared dividend. Quistclose lent the money specifically for that purpose, paid into a separate Barclays account. Rolls Razor went into liquidation before the dividend was paid. The House of Lords held the money was held on trust for Quistclose throughout and did not pass to the general creditors.
Quistclose Trusts.conclusion: The Quistclose trust is directly relevant to solicitors' accounts: client money received for a specific transaction purpose (e.g. to fund a purchase or pay a particular liability) may be held on trust terms analogous to Quistclose. If the purpose fails, the money must be returned to the client and cannot be absorbed into the firm's general assets.
Quistclose Trusts.summary_line: QUISTCLOSE TRUST · PURPOSE TRUST · RESULTING TRUST · CLIENT MONEY · INSOLVENCY PRIORITY

Quistclose Trusts.issue: Whether a solicitor who received mortgage advance money subject to an undertaking to apply it only for property acquisition held it on Quistclose trust, and whether paying it away in breach of that undertaking gave rise to liability for dishonest assistance.
Quistclose Trusts.rule: Confirmed the Quistclose trust: money advanced for a specific purpose is held on trust for the lender until applied for that purpose. A solicitor who receives money subject to a purpose undertaking is a trustee of that money. Dishonest assistance in breach of trust requires a state of mind that is dishonest by the standards of ordinary, honest people (objective test, subjectively applied).
Quistclose Trusts.application: Leach (solicitor) received a loan advance from Twinsectra on an undertaking to use it solely for property acquisition by Yardley. He paid it to Yardley's own solicitor, who disbursed it for other purposes. The House of Lords held that a Quistclose trust arose on receipt. Leach was in breach of his undertaking and liable.
Quistclose Trusts.conclusion: Solicitors who receive client or third-party money subject to specific conditions hold it on trust and must not release it outside those conditions. Breach of a purpose undertaking is a breach of trust, exposing the solicitor to personal liability. The case reinforces why conditional funds (e.g. mortgage advances, staged payments) must be controlled strictly under the client account rules.
Quistclose Trusts.summary_line: QUISTCLOSE TRUST · SOLICITOR UNDERTAKING · CONDITIONAL RELEASE · DISHONEST ASSISTANCE · PERSONAL LIABILITY

Quistclose Trusts.front: Going deeper: Quistclose trust — theoretical foundations and contested analysis
Quistclose Trusts.answer: The Quistclose trust (Barclays Bank v Quistclose [1970]) has generated more academic controversy than almost any other English trust law concept. Lord Millett's rationalisation in Twinsectra v Yardley [2002] — that a primary trust arises in favour of the stated purpose, with a secondary resulting trust in favour of the lender if the purpose fails — was welcomed for its doctrinal coherence but criticised for its consequences. Professor William Swadling (Oxford) argued that treating a Quistclose arrangement as a trust creates severe conceptual difficulties: if the borrower holds on trust, they have no beneficial ownership and cannot grant security over the funds, yet commercial practice assumes precisely the opposite. Professor Roy Goode contended that the result is better explained as a form of conditional transfer, not a trust at all. For solicitors, the tension is acute: the Quistclose analysis means client money advanced for a specific transaction purpose may be held on trust terms that restrict the firm's freedom to apply it — even where the client has consented to broad terms of engagement. The unresolved question is whether purpose trusts can validly arise in purely commercial/professional relationships where no charitable or other recognised purpose exists.

Definition and Classification.front: A client sends a cheque for £1,500 to cover anticipated counsel's fees and court fees in pending litigation. The disbursements have not yet been incurred. Is this money client money, and where must it be held?
Definition and Classification.answer: Yes — this is client money. Under rule 2.1 of the SRA Accounts Rules 2019, money received from a client in connection with regulated services is client money. Because the disbursements are anticipated but not yet incurred, the £1,500 is being held on account of future disbursements, not as reimbursement of an existing debt. It must be paid promptly into the client account (rule 2.3) and held there until properly applied.
Definition and Classification.summary_line: CLIENT MONEY · ANTICIPATED DISBURSEMENTS · CLIENT ACCOUNT · RULE 2.1

Definition and Classification.front: A client sends a cheque for £900 in response to a bill that the solicitor rendered last week for completed conveyancing work. Is this client money?
Definition and Classification.answer: No — this is not client money. Payment of a bill already rendered is money due to the firm, not money held for or on behalf of the client. It is office money and must be paid into the office account, not the client account. Paying it into the client account would be a technical breach of rule 2.2, which requires that money that belongs to the firm not be held in the client account.
Definition and Classification.summary_line: OFFICE MONEY · PAYMENT OF BILL · NOT CLIENT MONEY · OFFICE ACCOUNT · RULE 2.2

Definition and Classification.front: In a personal injury claim, an insurer pays £35,000 directly into the court and the court holds the funds pending distribution. The solicitor never receives or handles the money. Does the solicitor's client account need to reflect this payment?
Definition and Classification.answer: No. The SRA Accounts Rules only apply to money that the solicitor receives, holds, or controls. Money paid directly by a third party to the court — and held by the court — is not client money in the solicitor's hands. The solicitor has no obligation to pass it through the client account. Once the court releases the funds to the solicitor, they become client money at that point and must be paid promptly into the client account.
Definition and Classification.summary_line: CLIENT MONEY · THIRD PARTY PAYMENT · COURT FUNDS · CONTROL TEST · RECEIPT TRIGGER

Definition and Classification.front: A new client pays a £500 general retainer 'to secure the solicitor's availability for future matters.' No specific matter has been identified. Is this client money, and how must the firm handle it?
Definition and Classification.answer: Yes — this is client money. A general retainer paid before any specific work is carried out is money held on behalf of the client, not money earned by the firm. It must be paid into the client account promptly (rule 2.3). It can only be transferred to the office account once specific work is done, a bill is rendered, and the client authorises the transfer (rule 5.1). Simply receiving a retainer does not entitle the firm to treat it as earned income.
Definition and Classification.summary_line: GENERAL RETAINER · CLIENT MONEY · CLIENT ACCOUNT · NOT EARNED INCOME · RULE 2.3

Definition and Classification.front: A solicitor receives a damages cheque for £42,000 made payable to the client. The solicitor endorses it and pays it into the client account. A separate costs order of £8,500 has been agreed. Identify what happens to each element.
Definition and Classification.answer: The full £42,000 is client money on receipt and must be held in the client account. Once the bill of costs (£8,500) is rendered and the client provides authority, the firm may transfer £8,500 from the client account to the office account (rule 5.1). The remaining £34,500 belongs to the client and must be paid to the client promptly after any agreed deductions. The firm must not hold the balance beyond the point when it is no longer needed — lingering client balances are a common accounts rule breach.
Definition and Classification.summary_line: DAMAGES RECEIPT · CLIENT MONEY · TRANSFER TO OFFICE · CLIENT AUTHORITY · PROMPT PAYMENT TO CLIENT

Definition and Classification.front: A solicitor's firm receives a mixed electronic payment of £6,000 from a buyer's solicitor: £4,000 represents the client's net sale proceeds and £2,000 is payment of the firm's costs and VAT already billed and agreed. How must the firm deal with this payment on receipt?
Definition and Classification.answer: The £6,000 is a mixed receipt. The £4,000 is client money and must go to the client account. The £2,000 is money owed to the firm (payment of an already-rendered bill) and is office money. Under rule 4.2, where a firm receives a mixed payment, it may pay the whole sum into the client account initially, but the office money element (£2,000) must be transferred out to the office account promptly — and in any event within 14 days. Alternatively, the mixed amount may be split on receipt.
Definition and Classification.summary_line: MIXED RECEIPT · CLIENT MONEY · OFFICE MONEY · 14-DAY RULE · RULE 4.2

Definition and Classification.front: A solicitor receives £3,200 from a client. £1,000 relates to an invoice rendered three days ago for completed litigation work; £800 is to reimburse a court fee the solicitor already paid from the office account; and £1,400 is an advance for future counsel's fees. Classify each element and state what must happen to each.
Definition and Classification.answer:
Three separate classifications apply:

£1,000 (payment of rendered bill): office money. The invoice was already rendered, so this is money owed to the firm. It must go to the office account.

£800 (reimbursement of disbursement already paid from office account): office money. Because the court fee was already incurred and funded by the firm, the reimbursement is a debt repayment to the firm, not money held for the client. It goes to the office account.

£1,400 (advance for future counsel's fees): client money. Because the disbursement has not yet been incurred, this is held on account for the client and must be paid into the client account promptly. It can only be applied when counsel's fee falls due.

Total to office account: £1,800. Total to client account: £1,400. If received as a single payment, the mixed receipt rules apply (rule 4.2) and the split must be completed within 14 days.
Definition and Classification.summary_line: MIXED RECEIPT · THREE-WAY SPLIT · REIMBURSEMENT · ADVANCE DISBURSEMENTS · RULE 4.2

Definition and Classification.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

A solicitor delivers a bill for £3,500 on a completed probate matter. Two days later, the client sends a cheque for £3,500. The bookkeeper, noting that the bill has been paid, pays the cheque into the client account for ease of processing, intending to transfer it to the office account later.
Definition and Classification.issue: The Trap: The bookkeeper treats the payment as client money because it arrived from the client, intending to sort out the allocation later. The source of the money (the client) is used as the classification test.
Definition and Classification.rule: The Reality: Payment of a rendered bill is money due to the firm. It is office money — not client money — and must go directly into the office account (rule 2.2). The source of money does not determine its classification; what matters is whether the money belongs to the client or to the firm. Once a bill has been delivered and the client pays it, those funds belong to the firm.
Definition and Classification.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The category 'client money' is defined by beneficial ownership and purpose — not by who sent it. A cheque from a client paying a bill is no different from a bank transfer from the same client for the same purpose: both are firm money. Paying it into the client account and 'transferring later' is a breach of rule 2.2 and creates a false client account balance.
Definition and Classification.conclusion: The Key Rule: Post-bill payment = office money = office account. Classification follows ownership, not source.
Definition and Classification.summary_line: POST-BILL PAYMENT · OFFICE MONEY · OFFICE ACCOUNT · RULE 2.2 · CLASSIFICATION BY OWNERSHIP

Definition and Classification.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

A buyer's solicitor receives a mixed electronic receipt of £105,000: £100,000 is the mortgage advance (client money) and £5,000 is the lender's contribution to the firm's costs (office money). To keep things simple, the entire £105,000 is paid into the client account.
Definition and Classification.issue: The Trap: Paying the whole mixed receipt into the client account is treated as a straightforward breach — 'you put office money in the client account, that's wrong.'
Definition and Classification.rule: The Reality: Under rule 4.2, paying a mixed receipt into the client account is expressly permitted as a method of handling a mixed payment. It is not a breach to do so, provided the office money element is transferred out to the office account promptly — and in any event within 14 days. The breach only arises if the firm fails to separate the office money within that window.
Definition and Classification.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The rule recognises the practical difficulty of splitting mixed receipts on arrival. The automatic assumption that paying everything into the client account is wrong conflates the act of receipt (permitted by rule 4.2) with a failure to segregate (only a breach if the 14-day deadline is missed). The 14-day window is critical — time starts running from receipt.
Definition and Classification.conclusion: The Key Rule: Mixed receipt → client account initially = permitted. Must transfer office money out within 14 days. Breach = failure to separate, not receipt itself.
Definition and Classification.summary_line: MIXED RECEIPT · RULE 4.2 · 14-DAY WINDOW · CLIENT ACCOUNT INITIALLY PERMITTED · BREACH ON FAILURE TO SEPARATE

Mixed Receipts.front: What is a 'mixed receipt' in the context of the SRA Accounts Rules 2019, and what is the 14-day rule?
Mixed Receipts.answer:
A mixed receipt is a single payment that contains both client money and office money. For example, a payment that includes the client's damages (client money) and the solicitor's profit costs already billed (office money) is a mixed receipt.

The 14-day rule (rule 4.2) allows a firm to pay the entire mixed receipt into the client account initially, but requires that any office money element be transferred to the office account within 14 days. This prevents money belonging to the firm from sitting in the client account indefinitely, which would inflate the client account balance and obscure the true position.
Mixed Receipts.summary_line: MIXED RECEIPT · 14-DAY RULE · RULE 4.2 · CLIENT MONEY + OFFICE MONEY · SPLIT REQUIRED

Mixed Receipts.front: A solicitor acts for a claimant in a road traffic accident. A cheque for £11,200 arrives from the defendant's insurer. This covers: the client's general damages of £8,000; the solicitor's fixed recoverable costs of £2,500; and disbursements already paid from the office account of £700. How must the firm deal with this payment?
Mixed Receipts.answer:
This is a mixed receipt requiring a three-way split:

£8,000 (client's damages): client money → client account.

£2,500 (solicitor's costs, if a bill has been rendered and the client has authority): office money → office account. If no bill has been rendered, this element must also go to client account and can only be transferred once a bill is issued and authority obtained.

£700 (reimbursement of disbursements already paid from office): office money → office account. This is repayment of a debt owed to the firm, not money held for the client.

If all elements are clearly identified, the firm can split them on receipt. Alternatively, the full £11,200 can go to client account with the office money elements (£3,200) transferred out within 14 days (rule 4.2).
Mixed Receipts.summary_line: MIXED RECEIPT · THREE-WAY SPLIT · DAMAGES · COSTS · DISBURSEMENT REIMBURSEMENT · RULE 4.2

Mixed Receipts.front: A solicitor receives a £20,000 settlement payment into the client account on 1 March. The breakdown is: £15,000 client damages; £4,000 solicitor's costs (bill rendered and client authority given); £1,000 VAT on costs. On 20 March no transfer has been made. Is this a breach?
Mixed Receipts.answer:
Yes — this is a breach of rule 4.2. The office money element (£4,000 costs + £1,000 VAT = £5,000) should have been transferred from the client account to the office account within 14 days of receipt. The 14-day period expired on 15 March. By 20 March, the firm has held £5,000 of office money in the client account for five days beyond the permitted period.

The transfer should be made immediately upon discovery. The breach should be recorded in the compliance log. If the overhang is identified by the reporting accountant, it will appear in the accountant's report and may be reportable to the SRA depending on its severity and whether it is part of a pattern.
Mixed Receipts.summary_line: 14-DAY RULE · BREACH · OFFICE MONEY IN CLIENT ACCOUNT · RULE 4.2 · ACCOUNTANT'S REPORT

Mixed Receipts.front: A commercial solicitor receives £9,500 from a counterparty in a contract dispute as a full and final settlement. The firm's agreed costs are £4,000 + VAT (£800). No bill has yet been rendered. What must the solicitor do with the £9,500?
Mixed Receipts.answer:
The full £9,500 is client money until a bill is rendered and client authority to transfer is obtained. Even though the costs element (£4,800) is ultimately owed to the firm, no bill has been issued, so it cannot yet be categorised as office money. The entire £9,500 must go into the client account (rule 2.3).

Next steps: the solicitor must promptly render a bill to the client for £4,000 + VAT and obtain written or express authority to transfer £4,800 from the client account to the office account. The balance of £4,700 (£9,500 − £4,800) belongs to the client and must be paid out to the client promptly.
Mixed Receipts.summary_line: NO BILL YET · ALL TO CLIENT ACCOUNT · RENDER BILL FIRST · THEN TRANSFER · RULE 5.1

Mixed Receipts.front: A solicitor receives £2,800 from a client. £1,800 is to repay a disbursement the solicitor paid from the office account last week. £1,000 is an advance for future counsel's fees. The solicitor pays the full £2,800 into client account. Is this correct, and what must happen next?
Mixed Receipts.answer:
Paying the full £2,800 into the client account is permitted as an interim step under rule 4.2 (mixed receipt). However:

£1,800 (reimbursement of disbursement already paid from office): this is office money — the firm has already incurred and funded the expense. It must be transferred from the client account to the office account within 14 days.

£1,000 (advance for future counsel's fees): this is client money and must remain in the client account until the disbursement is properly incurred.

Action: transfer £1,800 to office account within 14 days. The £1,000 stays in the client account pending the instruction to brief counsel.
Mixed Receipts.summary_line: REIMBURSEMENT = OFFICE MONEY · ADVANCE = CLIENT MONEY · 14 DAYS · RULE 4.2 · SPLIT

Mixed Receipts.front: A solicitor acting in a residential sale receives a single electronic transfer of £285,000 on completion day. The breakdown is: net sale proceeds to client £265,000; solicitor's costs £3,500 + VAT £700; estate agent's fee £14,500 (to be paid from proceeds); SDLT redemption from proceeds £1,300. A bill has been rendered and client authority given. Set out in full how the solicitor must allocate and pay away each element.
Mixed Receipts.answer:
All £285,000 is received into client account. Then:

1. £4,200 (costs + VAT, bill rendered, authority given): transfer to office account under rule 5.1.

2. £14,500 (estate agent's fee): pay directly to the estate agent from client account — this is a disbursement on the client's behalf (rule 5.1(c)). The solicitor must hold a cleared authority or instruction from the client to make this payment.

3. £1,300 (SDLT redemption if applicable): pay to HMRC from client account as a disbursement — only where properly due and authorised.

4. £265,000 (net proceeds): pay to client promptly.

The solicitor must not retain any balance in client account once all payments are made. Each payment must be supported by the corresponding authority and recorded on the client ledger. A residual balance of zero should be confirmed at completion of all payments.
Mixed Receipts.summary_line: COMPLETION DAY · MULTI-ELEMENT ALLOCATION · DISBURSEMENTS · RULE 5.1 · ZERO RESIDUAL

Obligation to hold client money in clien.subtopic: Obligation to hold client money in client account / prompt payment
Obligation to hold client money in clien.front: What is the fundamental obligation regarding holding client money in a client account?
Obligation to hold client money in clien.answer:
All client money must be held in a client account at an authorised institution. A client account is a bank or building society account held in the firm's name, clearly designated as a client account in its title. Client money must not be held in the office account, in a personal account, or in any account not designated as a client account.

⚠️ Failure to maintain a separate designated client account is a fundamental breach of the Rules and exposes clients to loss on the firm's insolvency.
Obligation to hold client money in clien.summary_line: CLIENT ACCOUNT · AUTHORISED INSTITUTION · DESIGNATED · SEPARATE · FUNDAMENTAL OBLIGATION

Obligation to hold client money in clien.subtopic: Obligation to hold client money in client account / prompt payment
Obligation to hold client money in clien.front: What does 'promptly' mean when paying client money into a client account under the SRA Accounts Rules 2019?
Obligation to hold client money in clien.answer:
'Promptly' is not defined in the Rules, but SRA guidance indicates it means the same day or, if not reasonably practicable, the next working day. The firm must not hold client money in the office account, in a desk drawer, or elsewhere while waiting for instructions.

⚠️ If the solicitor expects the money to be paid out immediately, the Rules still generally require it to pass through the client account first (unless it is immediately directed to a third party as part of the transaction).
Obligation to hold client money in clien.summary_line: PROMPT · SAME DAY / NEXT WORKING DAY · SRA GUIDANCE · NO DELAY · CLIENT ACCOUNT FIRST

Obligation to hold client money in clien.subtopic: Obligation to hold client money in client account / prompt payment
Obligation to hold client money in clien.front: In what circumstances may client money be held outside a client account?
Obligation to hold client money in clien.answer:
Client money may be held outside a client account only in narrow circumstances:

1. the client specifically instructs the firm to hold it elsewhere (e.g., in a TPMA)
2. it is held under a valid TPMA arrangement with an FCA-authorised provider; or
3. money passes directly to a third party as part of the matter and is never held by the firm.

⚠️ Holding client money in the office account on the basis that it is more convenient, or that it will be paid out the same day, is not a permitted exception.
Obligation to hold client money in clien.summary_line: NARROW EXCEPTIONS · CLIENT INSTRUCTION · TPMA · PASS-THROUGH · NOT OFFICE ACCOUNT FOR CONVENIENCE

Obligation to hold client money in clien.subtopic: Obligation to hold client money in client account / prompt payment
Obligation to hold client money in clien.front: What type of institution must a client account be held at under the SRA Accounts Rules?
Obligation to hold client money in clien.answer:
A client account must be held at an 'authorised institution' — a bank or building society authorised by the Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) and regulated by the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA). The firm must verify the institution is currently authorised.

⚠️ Holding client money with an unauthorised institution is a breach even if the money is otherwise safe. The Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS) protects client accounts up to £85,000 per institution per beneficial owner, which is relevant for large balances.
Obligation to hold client money in clien.summary_line: AUTHORISED INSTITUTION · PRA/FCA · BANK OR BUILDING SOCIETY · VERIFY AUTHORISATION · FSCS £85,000

Obligation to hold client money in clien.subtopic: Obligation to hold client money in client account / prompt payment
Obligation to hold client money in clien.front: A firm receives client money by electronic transfer at 4:30 pm on a Friday. When must it be paid into the client account?
Obligation to hold client money in clien.answer:
Promptly — the same day if reasonably practicable, or the next working day (Monday) if not. The firm must not hold the money in the office account over the weekend. Online banking should be used to transfer to the client account if same-day branch banking is unavailable.

⚠️ 'Banking hours have closed' is not a valid reason to delay — if online banking is available, the transfer should be made that evening.
Obligation to hold client money in clien.summary_line: FRIDAY RECEIPT · SAME DAY OR MONDAY · ONLINE BANKING · NO DELAY · WEEKEND IN OFFICE ACCOUNT = BREACH

Withdrawals from client account — when p.subtopic: Withdrawals from client account — when permitted
Withdrawals from client account — when p.front: Set out the framework for when a firm is permitted to withdraw money from the client account.
Withdrawals from client account — when p.answer:
Withdrawals from client account are permitted only in the following circumstances:

1. to make a payment on behalf of the client for a purpose for which the money was received
2. to pay costs properly due to the firm after a bill has been delivered
3. to return client money to the client or original payer
4. to pay money to a third party where the client has authorised this
5. to correct an error.

⚠️ Any withdrawal for a purpose not on this list is unauthorised and constitutes a breach — potentially amounting to misappropriation.
Withdrawals from client account — when p.summary_line: FIVE PERMITTED WITHDRAWALS · AUTHORISED PURPOSE · DELIVERED BILL · RETURN TO CLIENT · ERROR CORRECTION

Withdrawals from client account — when p.subtopic: Withdrawals from client account — when permitted
Withdrawals from client account — when p.front: Can a firm withdraw money from the client account to pay its own costs before delivering a bill to the client?
Withdrawals from client account — when p.answer:
No. A firm may only withdraw money from the client account to pay its own costs after a bill has been properly delivered to the client. Withdrawing costs without a delivered bill is an unauthorised withdrawal and a breach.

⚠️ A 'bill' for this purpose must be a valid statute bill complying with the Solicitors Act 1974 — a breakdown or informal email summary will not suffice. The bill must clearly set out the costs claimed and be signed by an authorised person.
Withdrawals from client account — when p.summary_line: NO BILL = NO WITHDRAWAL · DELIVERED BILL REQUIRED · STATUTE BILL · SOLICITORS ACT 1974 · BREACH

Withdrawals from client account — when p.subtopic: Withdrawals from client account — when permitted
Withdrawals from client account — when p.front: A firm paid search fees of £300 from the office account last week on behalf of a client. Can it now transfer £300 from client account to reimburse the office account?
Withdrawals from client account — when p.answer:
Yes. The firm may transfer money from client account to reimburse itself for disbursements actually paid from the office account on behalf of the client, provided:

1. the disbursement has actually been paid; and
2. the client provided money for that purpose (or the firm has authority to withdraw for that purpose).

⚠️ The firm cannot withdraw client money to reimburse itself for anticipated disbursements not yet incurred — only for those actually paid.
Withdrawals from client account — when p.summary_line: REIMBURSEMENT · DISBURSEMENT ACTUALLY PAID · FROM OFFICE ACCOUNT · TRANSFER PERMITTED · NOT ANTICIPATED

Withdrawals from client account — when p.subtopic: Withdrawals from client account — when permitted
Withdrawals from client account — when p.front: The firm pays counsel's brief fee of £4,000 from the office account. The client has not yet been billed for this. Can the firm transfer £4,000 from client account to reimburse the office account?
Withdrawals from client account — when p.answer:
Yes, provided:

1. the disbursement (counsel's fee) has actually been paid
2. the client provided money for that purpose (e.g., a payment on account is held in client account); and
3. the transfer is for the correct amount.

The firm need not deliver a final bill before reimbursing itself for a specific disbursement already paid — disbursements are not profit costs.

⚠️ Contrast with profit costs: these require a delivered bill before transfer from client to office account. Do not conflate disbursements with costs.
Withdrawals from client account — when p.summary_line: COUNSEL'S FEE = DISBURSEMENT · ACTUALLY PAID · TRANSFER PERMITTED · NO BILL NEEDED · CONTRAST WITH PROFIT COSTS

Withdrawals from client account — when p.subtopic: Withdrawals from client account — when permitted
Withdrawals from client account — when p.front: Gap-fill: A firm may withdraw money from client account to pay its own costs only after _______ has been delivered to the client. Without this, the withdrawal is _______ and may constitute _______ of client money.
Withdrawals from client account — when p.answer:
A firm may withdraw money from client account to pay its own costs only after a bill has been delivered to the client. Without this, the withdrawal is unauthorised and may constitute misappropriation of client money.

⚠️ This is one of the most frequently tested points in SQE1 Solicitors' Accounts — always ask: has a bill been delivered?
Withdrawals from client account — when p.summary_line: DELIVERED BILL · UNAUTHORISED · MISAPPROPRIATION · COSTS · FREQUENTLY TESTED

Withdrawals from client account — when p.subtopic: Withdrawals from client account — when permitted
Withdrawals from client account — when p.front: A solicitor mistakenly withdraws £1,000 from the client account to pay the firm's electricity bill. What must happen?
Withdrawals from client account — when p.answer:
The shortfall must be made good immediately by paying £1,000 from the office account back into the client account. The client account must not be left in deficit at any time. The error must be corrected as soon as it is discovered.

⚠️ A client account shortage — where the firm holds less than it should for clients — is one of the most serious breaches under the Rules. If the shortfall cannot be immediately corrected (e.g., the firm lacks office funds), this must be reported to the SRA.
Withdrawals from client account — when p.summary_line: SHORTAGE · MAKE GOOD IMMEDIATELY · OFFICE FUNDS TO CLIENT ACCOUNT · RECORD ERROR · REPORT IF NOT CORRECTABLE

Returning client money.front: When must a firm return client money to its client?
Returning client money.answer:
A firm must return client money promptly once it is no longer needed for the purpose for which it was held. Occasions include:

1. the matter concludes
2. the client terminates the retainer
3. an anticipated payment (e.g., a disbursement) does not materialise
4. the client requests return.

The obligation arises immediately on any of these events — there is no discretion to retain money once the purpose is fulfilled.

⚠️ Retaining client money beyond the point of need is a breach, even if no dishonesty is intended.
Returning client money.summary_line: PROMPT RETURN · MATTER ENDS · RETAINER TERMINATED · DISBURSEMENT NOT NEEDED · CLIENT REQUEST · NO DISCRETION

Returning client money.front: Mid-way through a transaction, a client requests return of a payment on account of £1,000. The firm has not yet incurred any costs. Must the firm return it?
Returning client money.answer:
Yes. If no costs or disbursements have been incurred, the firm holds the £1,000 on trust for the client and must return it. The client owns the money beneficially and may withdraw a payment on account at any time (subject to any contractual terms about termination costs).

⚠️ The firm cannot withhold client money as 'security' for anticipated future costs — only money already applied to a delivered bill or actual disbursements may be retained.
Returning client money.summary_line: RETURN IMMEDIATELY · HOLDS ON TRUST · NO COSTS INCURRED · CANNOT RETAIN AS SECURITY · BENEFICIAL OWNERSHIP

Returning client money.front: The firm receives a bank transfer of £10,000 into its client account which the client confirms was sent in error (they meant to pay a third party). What must the firm do?
Returning client money.answer:
The firm must return the money to the client promptly. While it is held by the firm, it is client money — the Accounts Rules apply. The firm must not deduct its own costs or fees from the mistaken payment without the client's consent.

⚠️ If there are competing claims to the money (e.g., a third party asserts entitlement), or if the firm has already spent it, the firm must take urgent legal advice and consider notifying the SRA.
Returning client money.summary_line: MISTAKE · RETURN PROMPTLY · CLIENT MONEY WHILE HELD · NO DEDUCTION WITHOUT CONSENT · SRA IF COMPETING CLAIMS

Returning client money.front: A client owes the firm £3,000 in unpaid fees. The firm holds £3,000 in client account from a concluded matter. Can the firm set off the balance against the unpaid fees without a bill?
Returning client money.answer:
No. The firm may not unilaterally set off client money against unpaid fees without:

1. a delivered bill for those fees; and
2. clear client authority (express or implied) to apply the balance.

Without a delivered bill, any withdrawal is unauthorised.

⚠️ The firm's remedy is to deliver a valid bill and then transfer the money with authority. If the client disputes the bill, the firm must pursue the debt through normal legal channels — it cannot 'help itself' to client money.
Returning client money.summary_line: NO SET-OFF WITHOUT BILL · DELIVERED BILL REQUIRED · CLIENT AUTHORITY · CANNOT HELP ITSELF · NORMAL DEBT ROUTE

Operation of client account and records.subtopic: Operation of client account and records
Operation of client account and records.front: What records must a firm maintain in relation to client accounts under the SRA Accounts Rules?
Operation of client account and records.answer:
A firm must maintain:

1. a client cash account (chronological record of all client account receipts and payments)
2. a client ledger for each client matter (showing the running balance)
3. a record of all transfers between client and office accounts
4. client bank statements.

All records must be accurate, contemporaneous, and retained for at least six years.

⚠️ Electronic records are acceptable provided they can be reproduced in legible form. Retrospective or reconstructed records are insufficient.
Operation of client account and records.summary_line: CASH ACCOUNT · CLIENT LEDGER · TRANSFERS · BANK STATEMENTS · SIX YEARS · CONTEMPORANEOUS

Operation of client account and records.subtopic: Operation of client account and records
Operation of client account and records.front: How frequently must a firm carry out a client account reconciliation, and what does the reconciliation involve?
Operation of client account and records.answer:
SRA guidance requires reconciliations at least every five weeks (some firms do monthly). A reconciliation checks:

1. the total of all individual client ledger balances equals the client cash account balance; and
2. the client cash account balance agrees with the bank statement balance (adjusted for uncleared items).

Any discrepancy must be investigated and resolved promptly.

⚠️ Failing to carry out regular reconciliations is itself a breach of the record-keeping rules.
Operation of client account and records.summary_line: EVERY 5 WEEKS · THREE-WAY CHECK · LEDGER BALANCES · CASH ACCOUNT · BANK STATEMENT · DISCREPANCY INVESTIGATE

Operation of client account and records.subtopic: Operation of client account and records
Operation of client account and records.front: What is the purpose of a client ledger account, and what must it never show?
Operation of client account and records.answer:
A client ledger records all money received and paid for a specific client matter, showing a running balance of client money held. It allows the firm to account to the client for every pound received and disbursed. Each matter typically has its own ledger.

⚠️ A client ledger must never show a debit balance — a debit balance indicates the firm holds less for that client than it should, which is a client account shortage. This is a serious breach requiring immediate rectification.
Operation of client account and records.summary_line: PER MATTER · RUNNING BALANCE · ACCOUNT TO CLIENT · DEBIT BALANCE = SHORTAGE · SERIOUS BREACH

Operation of client account and records.subtopic: Operation of client account and records
Operation of client account and records.front: On reconciliation, the firm finds that total client ledger balances (£52,400) exceed the client cash account balance (£51,200). What does this mean and what must the firm do?
Operation of client account and records.answer:
This reveals a client account shortage of £1,200 — the firm holds £1,200 less than its records show it owes to clients. This is a serious breach. Immediate steps:

1. investigate the cause (error, fraud, or misappropriation)
2. make good the shortfall from office funds without delay
3. consider whether SRA notification is required.

⚠️ If the shortfall cannot be immediately corrected, the SRA must be notified. Do not delay investigation or correction under any circumstances.
Operation of client account and records.summary_line: SHORTAGE £1,200 · LEDGER > CASH ACCOUNT · SERIOUS BREACH · MAKE GOOD IMMEDIATELY · INVESTIGATE · SRA

Operation of client account and records.subtopic: Operation of client account and records
Operation of client account and records.front: What obligations arise when a firm closes a client account?
Operation of client account and records.answer:
Before closing a client account, the firm must ensure:

1. all client money has been returned to clients or transferred to another designated client account
2. all client ledger balances are reconciled to zero
3. proper records including the closing bank statement are retained.

⚠️ A firm must not close a client account while any client money remains in it unless the money is transferred simultaneously to another client account. Residual balances remaining after closure efforts must be dealt with under the residual balance rules.
Operation of client account and records.summary_line: CLOSE ACCOUNT · ZERO BALANCES · RECONCILE · CLOSING STATEMENT · RESIDUAL BALANCE RULES APPLY

Breach of Trust and Liability.front: Target Holdings Ltd v Redferns [1996] AC 421
Breach of Trust and Liability.issue: Whether a solicitor who paid away mortgage advance funds in breach of trust was liable to restore the full sum to the client account, even though the transaction subsequently completed and the loss flowed from the overvalued security rather than the breach.
Breach of Trust and Liability.rule: A trustee who commits a breach of trust must restore the trust fund to the position it would have been in but for the breach. However, once the trust has been performed (the principal transaction has completed), the beneficiary's remedy is equitable compensation — assessed on the same causation and remoteness principles as common law damages. The court will not award compensation exceeding the loss actually caused by the breach.
Breach of Trust and Liability.application: Redferns (solicitors) paid mortgage advance money to the vendor's solicitor before obtaining the legal charge, in breach of Target's instructions. The transaction later completed, but the property was grossly overvalued and Target suffered significant loss. The House of Lords held that Target could not recover the full advance: the loss was caused by the bad security, not the premature payment.
Breach of Trust and Liability.conclusion: A solicitor's liability for breach of client account rules is not automatic full restoration of the sum paid away. Once the transaction has completed, equitable compensation is assessed with reference to causation: the solicitor is liable for loss actually attributable to the breach, not for losses that would have occurred regardless. This limits — but does not eliminate — liability for premature or unauthorised release of client funds.
Breach of Trust and Liability.summary_line: BREACH OF TRUST · EQUITABLE COMPENSATION · CAUSATION · MORTGAGE ADVANCE · PREMATURE RELEASE

Release of Mortgage Funds.front: Portman Building Society v Hamlyn Taylor Neck [1998] 4 All ER 202
Release of Mortgage Funds.issue: Whether a solicitor acting for both lender and borrower held the mortgage advance on trust for the lender, and whether releasing it without satisfying the conditions in the lender's instructions gave rise to strict liability for breach of trust.
Release of Mortgage Funds.rule: A solicitor who receives mortgage advance money from a lender with specific instructions as to its release holds that money on trust for the lender. Any departure from those instructions — even if the solicitor acts in good faith — constitutes a breach of trust for which the solicitor is strictly liable. The solicitor's authority to release the funds is limited to the exact terms of the lender's instructions.
Release of Mortgage Funds.application: Hamlyn Taylor Neck received the advance from Portman and released it to the vendor without ensuring that all conditions in Portman's instructions were satisfied. The Court of Appeal held the solicitors were strictly liable to repay the full advance: they had acted outside their actual authority and in breach of the trust on which the money was held.
Release of Mortgage Funds.conclusion: Solicitors acting for lenders in conveyancing transactions must comply strictly with lender instructions before releasing funds. There is no discretion: good faith is not a defence. This principle underpins the SRA Accounts Rules requirement that client money be released only when properly authorised, and reflects the solicitor's role as trustee of funds pending completion.
Release of Mortgage Funds.summary_line: LENDER INSTRUCTIONS · STRICT LIABILITY · BREACH OF TRUST · MORTGAGE ADVANCE · ACTUAL AUTHORITY

Prompt Payment into Client Account.front: A solicitor receives a client cheque for £5,000 on a Wednesday afternoon. The firm's bank closes at 3 pm and it is now 4 pm. By when must the cheque be paid into the client account, and what rule applies?
Prompt Payment into Client Account.answer: Rule 2.3 of the SRA Accounts Rules 2019 requires that client money be paid into a client account 'promptly.' The SRA has stated that 'promptly' means the same day if reasonably practicable, or the next working day if not. Because the bank has closed, it is not reasonably practicable to pay on Wednesday. The cheque must be paid in on Thursday — the next working day. Holding client money overnight is acceptable in this circumstance, but any further delay without justification would breach rule 2.3.
Prompt Payment into Client Account.summary_line: PROMPT PAYMENT · SAME DAY / NEXT WORKING DAY · CLIENT ACCOUNT · RULE 2.3

Prompt Payment into Client Account.front: A solicitor receives a client money transfer of £12,000 on a Friday at 5 pm. The firm's online banking allows same-day transfers at any time. The supervising partner says they will deal with it on Monday. Is this acceptable?
Prompt Payment into Client Account.answer: No — this is almost certainly a breach of rule 2.3. The rule requires client money to be paid into the client account 'promptly.' Because same-day electronic transfer is available, the bank closing time argument does not apply. The money can be transferred on Friday evening. Deferring until Monday without a compelling reason means client money is sitting outside the client account over a weekend, which is not prompt. The SRA expects firms to make use of available banking facilities to comply with the promptness obligation.
Prompt Payment into Client Account.summary_line: PROMPT PAYMENT · SAME DAY TRANSFER · RULE 2.3 · ELECTRONIC BANKING · BREACH

Withdrawals from Client Account.front: A solicitor wishes to transfer £3,000 from the client account to the office account to pay their outstanding fees. The client has not been asked. Is this permitted?
Withdrawals from Client Account.answer: No — this is a breach of rule 5.1. A solicitor may only withdraw money from the client account in limited circumstances, including when a bill has been rendered and the client has given specific authority, or when the money is otherwise properly due to the firm. Withdrawing fees without the client's authority (and without having first issued a bill) constitutes misuse of the client account. This exposes the solicitor to regulatory sanction and, in serious cases, intervention by the SRA.
Withdrawals from Client Account.summary_line: WITHDRAWAL · CLIENT AUTHORITY REQUIRED · BILL FIRST · RULE 5.1 · BREACH

Withdrawals from Client Account.front: A solicitor has completed a matter, rendered a bill for £1,800 (plus VAT of £360), and received the client's written authority to transfer the amount from the client account balance of £2,500. What entries are required on the client ledger, and what is the residual balance?
Withdrawals from Client Account.answer:
The following entries are required:

Client account ledger — Matter A:
  Opening balance (CR):  £2,500
  Transfer to office — costs (DR): £1,800
  Transfer to office — VAT (DR):    £360
  Closing client account balance:   £340 (CR)

Office account — Matter A:
  Costs received (CR):  £1,800
  VAT received (CR):      £360

The residual £340 in the client account belongs to the client and must be returned promptly. It must not remain in the client account once the matter is concluded — retaining it without reason would breach rule 2.5.
Withdrawals from Client Account.summary_line: TRANSFER TO OFFICE · BILL AND AUTHORITY · RESIDUAL BALANCE · RULE 5.1 · RULE 2.5

Withdrawals from Client Account.front: A solicitor anticipates that a search fee of £250 will be required next week. The client account holds £3,000 for this matter. The solicitor draws £250 from the client account today to have the cash ready. Is this permitted?
Withdrawals from Client Account.answer: No — this is a breach. Under rule 5.1(c), money can only be withdrawn from the client account to pay a disbursement that is 'properly required.' A disbursement is not properly required until it has actually been incurred. Drawing funds in anticipation of a future disbursement — before it falls due — is a misuse of client money. The correct approach is to pay the search fee when it arises: either from the client account at that point, or from the office account with reimbursement from the client account afterwards.
Withdrawals from Client Account.summary_line: ANTICIPATED DISBURSEMENT · NOT PROPERLY REQUIRED · RULE 5.1(c) · BREACH · TIMING

Withdrawals from Client Account.front: A solicitor draws £2,500 in costs from the client account after rendering a bill. The client subsequently queries the bill and the solicitor reduces the fee to £1,800. What is the solicitor's obligation regarding the £700 overpayment?
Withdrawals from Client Account.answer: The solicitor must replace the £700 overpayment to the client account immediately and without delay (rule 6.1). The overpayment creates a shortfall in the client account: the firm has taken more client money than it was entitled to. This is a breach of rule 5.1 (which only permits withdrawal of amounts properly due). The firm must also issue a revised bill for £1,800. If the overpayment is not corrected promptly, the breach becomes more serious and may need to be disclosed to the SRA and reported in the accountant's report.
Withdrawals from Client Account.summary_line: OVERPAYMENT · SHORTFALL · RULE 6.1 · REPLACE PROMPTLY · REVISED BILL

Withdrawals from Client Account.front: A solicitor acts for 30 claimants in a group litigation. A single settlement cheque for £450,000 is paid into the client account. The solicitor then transfers £90,000 to the office account to cover her costs without first sending individual bills to each claimant. Three claimants later dispute the deduction. What rules are engaged and what is the solicitor's exposure?
Withdrawals from Client Account.answer:
Rule 5.1 requires that a withdrawal from the client account to pay costs must be preceded by a bill being rendered and the client's (or clients') authority to transfer. Transferring £90,000 without individual bills and individual authority from 30 clients breaches rule 5.1.

Additionally, in a multi-client matter, the solicitor must maintain individual ledger accounts for each claimant's share (rule 8.2). Treating £450,000 as a single undifferentiated fund risks breaching the requirement to account for each client separately.

Exposure: the three disputing claimants may bring a complaint; the overpayment must be replaced immediately (rule 6.1). The SRA could investigate the inadequate billing and withdrawal process. The solicitor should issue itemised bills to each claimant immediately and seek retrospective authority, or return the full £90,000 to client account pending proper process.
Withdrawals from Client Account.summary_line: MULTI-CLIENT FUND · INDIVIDUAL LEDGERS · RULE 5.1 · BILL FIRST · RULE 8.2

Withdrawals from Client Account.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

A solicitor is about to pay counsel's brief fee of £2,000 from client account on the day before a hearing. The client account for this matter holds £500. The solicitor pays the full £2,000 from the client account, creating a shortfall of £1,500. She notes: 'I'll top it up from office account after the hearing — the client will pay us back.'
Withdrawals from Client Account.issue: The Trap: The solicitor treats the disbursement as urgent and assumes that the intention to make good the shortfall promptly — and the fact that the client will reimburse — cures the breach. The disbursement is treated as the priority; the accounting rules as secondary.
Withdrawals from Client Account.rule: The Reality: A solicitor may not create a deficit on a client matter ledger. Paying a disbursement that causes the client account balance for a matter to go into deficit is a breach of the Accounts Rules (rule 5.3). If insufficient client money is held, the solicitor must either pay the disbursement from the office account (as a disbursement on behalf of the client, recorded as a debt due from the client) or obtain additional funds from the client before making the payment.
Withdrawals from Client Account.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The intention to top up later does not cure the breach — the breach occurs the moment the deficit is created. The fact that the client will repay is also irrelevant to the Accounts Rules analysis: the Rules prohibit the use of one client's money to fund another client's disbursement, and a deficit effectively does exactly that (if the pooled account is used to cover the shortfall).
Withdrawals from Client Account.conclusion: The Key Rule: Never create a client matter deficit. Pay disbursements from office account if client funds are insufficient. Top-up intention ≠ compliance.
Withdrawals from Client Account.summary_line: NO DEFICIT · RULE 5.3 · PAY FROM OFFICE IF INSUFFICIENT · BREACH ON DEFICIT CREATION · INTENTION IRRELEVANT
